description: Step-by-step craft procedures for pipe fitting, structural steel erection, and equipment setting

steps: 1. Verify isometric drawing (ISO) is current revision — check AFC stamp
2. Check bill of materials — confirm all pipe, fittings, flanges, gaskets, bolts on hand
3. Verify material markings match MTR/CMTR and isometric requirements (size, schedule, material grade, heat number)
4. Cut pipe to length — add root gap allowance for each butt weld (typically 1/16 to 3/32 inch per weld)
5. Bevel pipe ends per WPS — 37.5 degree bevel is standard (75 degree included angle) with 1/16 inch root face
6. Clean bevel and 1 inch back from each side — remove oil, scale, paint, galvanizing
7. Fit pipe using alignment clamps (external or internal depending on size and access)
8. Check root gap with gap gauge — must match WPS (typically 1/16 to 3/32 for GTAW root, 3/32 to 1/8 for SMAW root)
9. Check hi-lo (internal misalignment) — maximum 1/16 inch per B31.3, less for some project specs
10. Level and plumb check — use torpedo level on pipe, measure from established reference points
11. Tack weld — minimum 4 tacks equally spaced for pipe over 6 inch diameter. Use qualified WPS and qualified welder.
12. Remove alignment clamp after tacking (for external clamp). For internal lineup clamp, remove per procedure.
13. Inspect tacks before welding — no cracks, proper size, ground to feather edges if incorporated into weld
14. Mark weld joint with welder ID stamp (downstream side or per project procedure)

alignment_methods.pipe_to_pipe: External chain clamp or strap clamp for same-size pipe. Jack screws for alignment. Wedges NOT recommended.
alignment_methods.pipe_to_fitting: Fit-up clamp or dogs (tack-welded alignment pieces — must be ground off and areas inspected after removal)
alignment_methods.pipe_to_flange: Flange must be perpendicular to pipe centerline. Check with straightedge and framing square. Flange bolt holes must straddle centerline (not sit on centerline) unless drawing says otherwise.
alignment_methods.large_bore_over_24_inch: Internal lineup clamp preferred. Hi-lo critical on large bore — small misalignment becomes large at weld root.
alignment_methods.small_bore_under_2_inch: Socket weld fittings common. Pipe must bottom out in socket, then back off 1/16 inch (gap prevents stress cracking). Use witness marks.

common_mistakes: Not checking drawing revision — welding to superseded drawing = rework
Wrong root gap — too tight means lack of penetration, too wide means burn-through or concavity
Hi-lo over tolerance — even 1/32 inch matters on high-integrity welds
Flange bolt holes not straddling centerline — entire spool must be redone
Not backing off socket weld 1/16 inch — thermal stress cracking in service
Forcing pipe into alignment with come-alongs — spring-loaded fit-up causes cracking and stress
Not cleaning bevels — contamination causes porosity and lack of fusion
Tack welds by unqualified welder — code violation, all tacks must be removed

requirements: Tacker must be qualified welder using qualified WPS
Preheat per WPS before tacking (same as production welding)
Minimum tack length: 1/2 inch for pipe under 2 inch, 1 inch for pipe 2-12 inch, 2 inches for pipe over 12 inch
Number of tacks: minimum 3 for pipe under 6 inch, minimum 4 for pipe 6-24 inch, 6-8 for pipe over 24 inch
Tacks must be free of defects — inspect visually before production welding
If tacks will be incorporated: grind start and stop to feather edge
If tacks will be removed: remove completely and inspect area before welding

steps: 1. Inspect flange faces — no damage, corrosion, scoring across sealing surface
2. Verify flange rating matches spec (check flange markings: 150, 300, 600, 900, 1500, 2500)
3. Verify gasket material and type match spec and flange type (RF gets spiral wound, RTJ gets ring gasket)
4. Clean flange faces thoroughly — wire brush radial serrations, remove old gasket material
5. Check flange alignment — parallel within 1/16 inch per foot of flange diameter, bolt holes aligned
6. Insert gasket — center properly. DO NOT use gasket paste/sealant unless specified.
7. Insert all bolts — nuts hand-tight. Verify correct bolt length (stud extends past nut by 1-3 threads minimum)
8. Number the bolts on the flange for tracking during torque sequence
9. Torque in star/cross pattern — 4 passes minimum:
Pass 1: 30% of target torque in star pattern
Pass 2: 60% of target torque in star pattern
Pass 3: 100% of target torque in star pattern
Pass 4: 100% of target torque going CLOCKWISE around the flange (rotational pass)
10. Apply witness marks (paint line across nut and flange) to verify no bolt relaxation
11. Document: joint number, torque value, gasket type, bolt spec, date, fitter/bolter ID

B7_studs_lubricated.note: These are APPROXIMATE values for B7 studs with anti-seize lubricant. ALWAYS use project-specified torque values when provided. Values vary significantly with lubricant type.

torque_values_ft_lbs.note_on_B8_stainless_bolts: B8 (304SS) and B8M (316SS) bolts have MUCH lower torque values than B7. Never use B7 torque values for B8 bolts. B8 Class 1 (solution annealed) is very low strength — commonly specified where process requires stainless.
torque_values_ft_lbs.note_on_L7_bolts: B7M and L7 bolts for low-temperature service. Similar torque to B7.
torque_values_ft_lbs.anti_seize: CRITICAL: Always use anti-seize/lubricant as specified. Dry bolts require 50-70% MORE torque for same clamp load. Wrong lubricant changes everything.
torque_values_ft_lbs.hydraulic_tensioning: Flanges over 2 inch stud diameter often use hydraulic bolt tensioners instead of torque wrenches. Target tension (not torque) is specified.

gasket_selection.spiral_wound_304_graphite: Standard for raised face flanges in hydrocarbon service. 304SS windings with flexible graphite filler. Inner ring prevents buckling, outer ring centers gasket.
gasket_selection.spiral_wound_316_graphite: For corrosive services — acids, chlorides. 316SS windings.
gasket_selection.ring_joint_RTJ: Metal ring for RTJ flanges — used in high pressure (typically 600# and above, always on 900#, 1500#, 2500#). Octagonal preferred over oval (better sealing). Material: soft iron, 304SS, 316SS as specified.
gasket_selection.sheet_PTFE: Low pressure, non-critical, chemical service. Not for hydrocarbons in refinery (fire risk — PTFE creeps).
gasket_selection.corrugated_metal_graphite: Heat exchanger flanges — conforms to wide sealing surfaces
gasket_selection.kammprofile: Premium gasket — corrugated metal core with graphite facing. Excellent for critical service, high bolt-load-to-gasket stress.
common_mistakes: Wrong gasket for flange type (spiral wound on RTJ flange = LEAK)
Spiral wound without inner ring on RF flange = gasket can buckle into pipe bore
Old gasket left on face — always use NEW gasket, NEVER reuse
Gasket not centered — uneven compression = leak
Wrong material for service — check piping spec class

flange_alignment_tolerances.parallelism: 1/16 inch per foot of flange OD (1/32 inch per foot for high integrity)
flange_alignment_tolerances.rotational_alignment: Bolt holes must be aligned — 1/8 inch max offset
flange_alignment_tolerances.gap: Flange gap should be uniform and equal to gasket thickness + 1/16 to 1/8 inch for gasket insertion
flange_alignment_tolerances.do_NOT: Never use bolts to pull flanges into alignment — this spring-loads the joint and causes leaks. Re-fit the spool.

steps: 1. Verify valve tag number matches line list / isometric
2. Verify valve rating, size, material, and type match specification
3. Inspect valve — no shipping damage, hand-operate to verify function, check seat seal
4. Remove shipping covers — inspect internals for debris
5. Verify flow direction arrow matches installation orientation
6. For gate and globe valves — install with stem vertical or above horizontal (never inverted unless specifically designed for it)
7. For check valves — verify flow direction, install per manufacturer orientation requirements
8. Support valve weight independently — do NOT hang pipe weight on valves (especially large valves)
9. Ensure valve handwheel/actuator is accessible for operation after insulation
10. For flanged valves — follow flange bolt-up procedure above
11. For butt-weld valves — protect internals during welding (remove soft seats, open gate/ball to protect seats from heat)
12. Leave valves in specified position for hydro test (typically open, check valves may need to be removed or bypassed)

special_considerations.ball_valves: Support on BOTH sides — heavy. Align actuator before final bolt-up. Full-port vs reduced-port matters for flow.
special_considerations.globe_valves: High pressure drop — install with pressure under disc (flow direction matters for valve operation and safety)
special_considerations.check_valves: Swing check needs horizontal or angled run (never vertical down). Piston check can be vertical. Dual-plate check needs to be oriented properly (hinge pin horizontal).
special_considerations.butterfly_valves: Disc must clear pipe bore — check gasket thickness doesn't protrude. Wafer style trapped between flanges.
special_considerations.control_valves: Instrument connection — coordinate with instrument tech. Install with actuator UP. Allow space for maintenance access.

steps: 1. Review expansion joint spec and piping flexibility analysis
2. Do NOT remove shipping bars/restraints until ready to install — expansion joints are fragile
3. Verify cold set (pre-compression or pre-extension) matches engineering specification
4. Install with proper anchoring — main anchors on each side of expansion joint must resist full pressure thrust force
5. Install pipe guides per manufacturer spacing requirements — first guide within 4 pipe diameters, second guide within 14 pipe diameters
6. Do NOT rotate or torque the expansion joint during installation
7. For bellows type — protect from weld spatter, impact, and arc strike (cover with fire blanket during nearby welding)
8. Remove shipping bars ONLY after all anchors and guides are complete and piping is fully supported
9. Document installed length (cold position) for commissioning reference
critical_notes: NEVER use expansion joints to make up for pipe misalignment — they are NOT alignment devices
Main anchors must resist pressure thrust force — this can be thousands of pounds. Under-designed anchors = catastrophic failure.
Pipe guides prevent lateral movement and buckling — missing/wrong guides cause bellows failure
Expansion joints have VERY limited cycle life — overstressing shortens life dramatically

rigid_supports.shoes: Standard pipe rest. Weld to pipe, set on steel support. Common sizes: 3-inch, 6-inch, 12-inch high.
rigid_supports.clamps: U-bolts, band clamps, riser clamps. Do NOT over-tighten — allow thermal movement unless fixed point.
rigid_supports.saddles: For large bore pipe. Distribute weight over larger arc of pipe surface.
rigid_supports.anchors: Fixed points — weld to pipe AND support. Prevents ALL movement at that point. Per flexibility analysis.
rigid_supports.guides: Prevents lateral movement but allows axial movement. Spider guides, channel guides.
rigid_supports.dummy_legs: Welded support leg, typically on elbows. Carries pipe weight to structural steel or foundation.

spring_supports.variable_spring: Most common spring hanger. Supports load that varies with thermal movement. MUST be set to cold load/position during installation.
spring_supports.constant_spring: Load remains constant through travel range. Used where load variation must be minimized (turbine connections, critical equipment nozzles).
installation_steps: 1. Verify spring tag number matches support drawing
2. Verify cold load setting matches hanger schedule
3. Install with travel stop pin/shipping pin IN PLACE
4. Verify spring scale indicator reads at cold position mark
5. After hydro test — check that spring can support hydro load (water-filled pipe is heavier)
6. Remove travel stop pins ONLY when directed during commissioning/startup
7. After system reaches operating temperature — verify spring indicator at hot position mark
8. Document: hanger tag, cold setting, hot reading, travel observed

snubbers.purpose: Dynamic restraints — allow slow thermal movement but resist rapid movement (seismic, water hammer, relief valve discharge)
snubbers.testing: Must be stroke-tested per manufacturer requirements before installation

general_rules: Support pipe per span tables — DO NOT exceed maximum pipe support spacing
Typical spans: 2-inch pipe = 10 ft, 4-inch = 14 ft, 6-inch = 17 ft, 8-inch = 19 ft, 12-inch = 23 ft, 16-inch = 27 ft, 24-inch = 32 ft (CS liquid service — check project tables for actual)
Support at EVERY change of direction (elbow, tee)
Support within 2 feet of every valve
Do NOT weld supports to alloy pipe without qualified WPS and preheat/PWHT as required
Insulation clearance — support must account for insulation thickness
Use pre-insulated pipe shoes on insulated lines to prevent heat bridges

steps: 1. Test package preparation:
- Identify test boundaries on P&ID and isometric
- Install test blinds at boundaries (rated for test pressure)
- Remove or isolate relief devices, instruments, expansion joints, check valves as required
- Verify ALL welds complete and NDE accepted
- Verify ALL supports and spring hanger pins in place
- Post test zone boundaries with signs and barricades
2. Fill system:
- Connect test pump and fill line at LOW POINT
- Open ALL high point vents
- Fill slowly from bottom up — this displaces air upward
- Close vents when SOLID water stream (no air bubbles) flows from each vent. Start with lowest vent, work up.
- CRITICAL: All air MUST be removed. Trapped air is dangerous and gives false readings.
3. Pressure up:
- Install calibrated test gauges (readable from outside barricade if possible, or remote digital gauge)
- Pressure up to 25% of test pressure — hold and inspect
- Pressure up to 50% of test pressure — hold and walk down all joints
- Continue to test pressure in increments
- If leak found: depressure completely, repair, restart sequence
4. Hold at test pressure:
- Minimum 10 minutes at full test pressure (many projects require 4 hours for leak tightness)
- Record pressure and temperature at start and end of hold
- Pressure will vary with temperature — account for thermal effects (sun on pipe causes pressure rise)
- Walk down ALL joints, welds, flanges — look for leaks, deformation, distortion
5. Document and release:
- Record all data: pressures, temperatures, times, leaks found, repairs made
- QC inspector and owner witness sign test report
- Depressure slowly through drain valve
- Remove test blinds and reinstall permanent blinds/spectacle blinds per P&ID
- Drain and dry system as required (especially stainless — chloride stress cracking from trapped water)
1. Review steam tracing drawings — identify tracer size, attachment method, supply/return headers
2. Typical tracer: 1/2 inch or 3/4 inch copper or stainless steel tubing
3. Run tracer straight along bottom or side of process pipe — secure every 3-6 feet with wire ties or bands
4. At valves: wrap tracer around valve body (3-4 wraps typically)
5. At flanges: loop tracer around flange pair
6. At instruments: loop tracer to maintain temperature at process connection
7. Maintain continuous fall toward condensate return (1/4 inch per foot minimum)
8. Install steam traps at low points and end of each tracer circuit
9. Test tracer circuits before insulation — verify flow through each tracer, check for leaks
10. Document tracer circuits for commissioning — tag each supply valve and trap
notes: Aluminum heat transfer compound increases efficiency at contact points
Tracer must be installed BEFORE insulation crews arrive
Number one problem: dead legs and condensate pooling from improper slope — causes water hammer

steps: 1. Pre-erection:
- Verify anchor bolts/base plates installed, surveyed, and grouted (if pre-grouted)
- Lay out steel delivery sequence to match erection plan
- Inspect all members — straightness, connection holes, shop welds, paint
- Verify bolt packages for each connection (number, size, grade)
2. Column erection:
- Set columns on anchor bolts/base plates first — these establish the grid
- Plumb columns using transit/theodolite — within 1:500 (L/500) plumbness tolerance per AISC
- Temporarily brace columns with cable guys or temporary bracing
- Tighten anchor bolt nuts, but allow for final plumbing adjustment
3. Primary beam erection:
- Set beams between columns — establishes structural bays
- Make connections (bolted or field-welded per design)
- For high-strength bolts: snug-tight, then turn-of-nut or tension-indicating methods
4. Secondary framing:
- Install bracing, girts, purlins, stair stringers
- Permanent bracing must be installed as erection progresses — cannot leave unbraced bays
5. Decking/grating:
- Install floor plate, grating, checker plate per drawings
- Tack weld or clip grating per spec
- Must provide safe walking surface for subsequent trades
6. Miscellaneous steel:
- Handrails, toe plates, ladders, cages, platform supports
- Pipe support steel — must be in place before piping can start
7. Field welding:
- All field welds per AWS D1.1 or project specification
- Inspect per ITP (Inspection and Test Plan)
8. Touch-up and final:
- Touch up paint/fireproofing damage from erection
- Final survey — verify dimensions, elevations, plumbness
- Punch list and turnover

bolt_tightening_methods.snug_tight: For common bolts and specified snug-tight connections. Defined as full effort of one ironworker with spud wrench.
bolt_tightening_methods.turn_of_nut: Snug tight first, then additional rotation: 1/3 turn for bolt length up to 4d, 1/2 turn for 4d-8d, 2/3 turn for 8d-12d. Mark with crayon before turning.
bolt_tightening_methods.calibrated_wrench: Torque wrench calibrated daily to achieve required tension
bolt_tightening_methods.tension_indicating: Direct tension indicating (DTI) washers — squeeze to specified gap, or tension-indicating bolts with splined tip that shears at target tension

bolt_grades.A325: 120 ksi tensile, most common structural bolt. Replaced by F3125 Grade A325.
bolt_grades.A490: 150 ksi tensile, heavy connections. Replaced by F3125 Grade A490. NEVER galvanize A490.
bolt_grades.A307: 60 ksi tensile, secondary connections, handrails, grating clips

AISC_tolerances.column_plumbness: 1/500 of height, max 1 inch total for multi-story
AISC_tolerances.beam_sweep: L/1000 or 1/4 inch, whichever is greater
AISC_tolerances.anchor_bolt_location: +/- 1/8 inch from center of bolt group

steps: 1. Planning:
- Get equipment weight from vendor data (include internals if shipped separate)
- Add rigging weight (slings, shackles, spreader bars, trunnions)
- Determine lift radius (center of crane rotation to center of gravity of load)
- Select crane based on load chart capacity at required radius
- For critical lifts (>75% capacity): develop written lift plan with engineer sign-off
2. Site preparation:
- Verify ground bearing capacity for crane (mats required if insufficient)
- Crane set-up area level within 1% slope
- Outriggers fully extended and on proper pads/mats
- Identify underground utilities — no outrigger loads on buried pipe/conduit
- Verify clear swing path — check overhead obstructions, power lines
3. Foundation readiness:
- Foundation survey complete — anchor bolts within tolerance
- Grout pads poured and cured (if pre-grouted design)
- Shim packs prepared to correct elevation
- Foundation ready letter from civil/structural engineer
4. Rigging:
- Select slings based on sling angle and load per leg
- All rigging inspected (current color code tag)
- Attach rigging per lift plan — rigging above center of gravity
- Minimum 2 tag lines — positioned to control rotation and swing
- Trial lift: lift 6-12 inches, hold, verify stability and balance
5. Set equipment:
- Lift and swing to position — slow and controlled
- Lower onto foundation — guide onto anchor bolts
- Set equipment level using precision level — shim to tolerance per vendor (typically 0.002 inch per foot for rotating equipment, 1/8 inch per 10 feet for vessels)
- Tighten anchor bolts to vendor specification
- For rotating equipment: preliminary alignment before releasing crane
6. Post-set:
- Remove rigging only after equipment secured
- Document: set date, weight, crane used, final elevation, level readings
- For rotating equipment: proceed to precision alignment
- For vessels/columns: check plumbness and orientation (nozzles in correct position)

common_equipment_weights.note: These are ROUGH estimates for planning. ALWAYS use actual vendor data for lift planning.
